Biological Technology in 2050

Silver Award Winner, The Economist/Shell World in 2050 Essay Competition

Rob Carlson
Copyright 2000, rcarlson@molsci.org

Summary

The potential impact upon industrial practices and the global economy of learning from biology is enormous.Ā Paying careful attention to several hundred million years of nature's trial and error design experience will provide considerable savings in energy and resources to human industry. Yet borrowing a design aesthetic for industrial function from nature is just the beginning. Beyond using biology as a model for the structure and function of industrial production, the year 2050 will see humans utilizing the living world as the means of production itself. This intentional biology will rely upon quantitative, predictive models of cells that will become de facto design tools. The combination of design tools, rapid and inexpensive DNA sequencing and synthesis, and the already widespread availability of information about laboratory techniques in molecular biology will create an environment where biological engineering will transition from research, to profession, to vocation, to avocation. In 2050, following the fine tradition of hacking automobiles and computers, garage biology hacking will be well underway.Ā Open-source biology will lead to distributed, renewable, biological manufacturing, which will be adopted early in developing economies as a way to bypass investment in traditional industrial infrastructure. The benefits and risks of this technology will create considerable public debate over the coming decades. But fear of potential hazards should be met with increased research and education rather than closing the door on the profound positive impacts open-source biological technology will have on human health, human impacts on the environment, and on increasing standards of living around the world. Technology based on intentional, open-source biology is on its way, whether we like it or not, and the opportunity it represents will just begin to emerge in the next fifty years.

Click here for the full essay as a pdf file (Acrobat Reader required).
